/publications/asymmetrical-pluralism-nor: Frontier chatbots are becoming everyday gatekeepers of "good" English, but they do not treat all varieties equally. This study uses critical discourse analysis on a corpus of interactions with GPT-5.1, Claude Opus 4.5, and Gemini 3 Pro across Singapore, Indian, Philippine, and Japanese English (alongside American and British norms). It shows how models often recast Asian English features as errors to fix, privileging globally dominant varieties in data and behavior—so many Englishes can appear in use while normative authority stays sharply uneven, a pattern the paper calls asymmetrical pluralism.
/publications/bilingual-chatbot-icaiic-2: Many Philippine call centers serve bilingual customers in Filipino, English, and mixed “Taglish,” but human-staffed chat is expensive to supervise. This paper builds a retail chatbot that can follow conversations in those languages. It learns from a dataset produced by an automatic bilingual corpus tool, then uses a support-vector classifier to detect what the customer wants and a simple hash-based check for slot-style details like product names.
/publications/covid-misinformation-tweet: During COVID-19, Filipinos turned to Twitter for news, which also spread rumors. The authors automatically pulled tweets that looked like misinformation using statistical scores (how informative vs. how “phrase-like” they were) and topic models on thousands of posts. Roughly three in four flagged tweets were actually misinformation by their test; clustering surfaced everyday themes—jokes, venting about government, prevention tips, and sharing awareness—not only blunt false claims.
/publications/covid-misinformation-twitt: Spotting COVID misinformation on Twitter is hard: computers scale but miss cultural nuance, while humans read deeply but cannot scan huge feeds. This mixed-methods study mined geotagged Philippine tweets from early 2020, used topic models and clinician-informed keywords to build smaller labeled sets, then tried NLP to scale those labels. They catalogued rumor formats (misleading framing, satire taken seriously, conspiracy, etc.) and tactics like humor or fearmongering, and showed that off-the-shelf NLP mislabeled many Filipino or mixed-language tweets—so local expertise stayed essential.
/publications/diabetic-retinopathy-mipr-: Diabetic eye disease can blind adults if caught late, yet the Philippines lacks a cheap national screening program, and clinic photos are often low-resolution. The paper proposes AffiDAVIT: augment fuzzy fundus images with a diffusion model, run a vision transformer for diagnosis, highlight where the model looks (heat maps), and use fuzzy logic when the model says “no disease” to reduce dangerous false negatives. On their benchmark it beat prior models and is framed as a more realistic path for underserved areas.
/publications/emotes-3k-nlp4dh-2023.pdf: AI that discusses right and wrong needs training data beyond English-only benchmarks. The authors released EMoTES-3K: thousands of parallel Tagalog and English mini-scenarios about moral actions, with labels and explanations. Fine-tuned models can classify those judgments fairly well and even generate explanations, though they still stumble when an action is morally mixed—setting a baseline for “commonsense morality” work in Filipino and related languages.
High-level AI ethics guidelines exist (EU AI Act, IEEE standards, NIST risk framework), but teams still struggle to turn values into day-to-day checks. This article lays out an “ethics-by-design” control stack across the whole ML lifecycle—data, training, deployment, monitoring—with three kinds of gates at each stage: numeric quality and safety metrics, legal and procedural compliance, and environmental budgets (energy, water). It maps those gates to concrete triggers, audits, and CI/CD hooks so ethics is closer to engineering than to slogans.
/publications/explaining-bias-cav-nldb-2: Large language models can sound biased even when the surface answer looks fine, because bias lives in internal activations, not only in the final string. The paper extends “concept activation vectors” into Bias-CAVs: linear probes trained on inner states from models like LLaMA, Mistral, Phi, and Gemma to separate stereotypical vs. neutral directions. Layer-by-layer plots show where gender, race, profession, or politics signals appear or get amplified—useful both for analysis and for more targeted debiasing.
/publications/floyd-warshall-dvrppd-ictc: Delivery routing gets harder when customers cancel mid-route; retraining a reinforcement-learning policy from scratch each time is slow. The authors introduce FLURRY, which precomputes all-pairs shortest paths between stops (classic Floyd–Warshall) in a lookup table, then patches only the affected entries in the Q-learning value matrix when a cancellation happens. On their dynamic pickup-delivery benchmark this reuse is several times faster than full retraining—and in a follow-up, sometimes even skipping initial Q-learning still worked when paired with FLURRY.
/publications/heat-iscmi-2022.pdf: Transformers—the architecture behind most modern language models—spend a lot of time comparing every token to every other token. HEAT replaces that expensive attention step with a cheaper approximation built from a low-rank Hadamard projection while trying to preserve accuracy. Experiments report faster training and inference than standard attention and competitive or better results versus other fast-attention variants on translation and classification tasks.
/publications/language-migration-chatgpt: Migrants increasingly use tools like ChatGPT for real-life tasks—filling forms, understanding doctors, everyday chat—where mistakes carry real consequences. The article argues these models both widen access (instant drafting, translation-like help) and introduce new risks: wrong advice, bias, impersonation, and opaque decisions, especially for people short on time or institutional support. Automation also pressures migrant-heavy service jobs. It closes by treating digital literacy about what ChatGPT can and cannot do as part of integration policy, not an optional extra.
/publications/metric-typology-gem-acl-20: Everyone agrees single leaderboard scores hide tradeoffs, but teams still talk past each other because metric designs are implicit. This ACL workshop paper proposes a small grammar for metrics—representation, distance, aggregation, and context—and shows how common scores (BLEU, BERTScore, nDCG, LLM-as-judge, calibration tests, agent benchmarks) are really different recipes in that grammar. It also stresses that metrics estimate fuzzy “constructs,” so picking a model should be multi-objective over those estimates, not argmax on one noisy number.
/publications/paclic-38-rerank-2024.pdf: Search and RAG pipelines need a second stage that sorts retrieved passages by relevance. The author asks whether a multiple-choice QA model—already trained to pick one best option among four—can be repurposed as that ranker. Building a prototype called R*, they draw on the math linking MCQA scoring to cross-encoder relevance and report accuracy gains while keeping the stack relatively lightweight.
/publications/parallel-corpus-filipino-t: Text-to-SQL lets non-experts query databases in natural language, but almost all public training data is English, and Filipino queries often code-switch. The team released iTanong: over sixteen thousand hand-checked Filipino question/SQL pairs from two universities, with POS tagging to study query shapes. A GPT-3 sanity test on a subset reached high execution and exact-match accuracy, suggesting the corpus is usable for real Filipino semantic parsing research.
/publications/speed-accuracy-sgd-iscmi-2: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is the workhorse for training neural nets but can be slow and noisy. This paper replaces the raw gradient step inside SGD with a smoother numerical approximation based on a fourth-order Runge–Kutta–Nyström ODE solver, creating “ASTRA” variants for gradient boosting and MLPs. The claim is faster convergence and lower variance without hurting final accuracy—i.e., a better speed–stability tradeoff on standard models.
/publications/text-to-sql-comparative-st: Natural-language-to-database-query research moves fast; this paper is a structured survey plus benchmark comparison. It explains how papers were selected, walks through each methodological family end-to-end, and scores models on a common dataset while also comparing design choices like pretrained encoders and intermediate representations. The takeaway is that recent gains mostly came from better encoders under a shared encoder–decoder template, while decoders and representation choices remain open design space.
/publications/thesis-proposal-multimodal: YouTube moderation often relies on English text only, struggles with code-switched kids’ content, and gives black-box scores. This ACL thesis proposal sketches a hybrid system: a fast multimodal classifier (video/audio/text) plus a large-language-model explainer that turns internal cues—like which words or image regions mattered—into short human-readable reasons. The plan centers an English–Filipino code-switched case study and a responsibly shared dataset (redacted media, agreements) to make child-safety tooling more transparent and equitable.
/publications/topic-modeling-vaccine-fac: Filipino Facebook chatter reflected vaccine anxiety during COVID; policymakers need to see topics and tone, not raw comment firehoses. The pipeline uses multilingual BERT for topics, several sentiment models with clustering, and embedding similarity to compare how vaccine brands look online. Findings: many threads skew negative; negativity spikes when the health department posts about other countries; heavy “laugh” reactions on serious posts suggest people are reacting to the poster, not the medical content—hinting that misinformation dynamics can emerge without a single false headline.
/publications/when-image-text-disagree-m: Multimodal RAG feeds language models both retrieved text and images; when those sources disagree, answers can collapse or hallucinate. CMC-Bench stress-tests open vision–language models with controlled conflicts (facts, time, entities, detail level) between modalities. Models lose a large chunk of accuracy under conflict, often “lean” on text even when the image is right, and lumping “refused to answer” together with “made something up” hides very different failure modes—so evaluations should track abstention versus fabrication separately.
